Supplementary Material for: Phases of itinerant anyons in Laughlin’s quantum Hall states on a lattice 

Tevˇ z Lotriˇ c and Steven H. Simon  

> I. RECOVERING THE FULL LOW-ENERGY STATE MANIFOLD WITH LOCALIZED QUASIHOLES STATES

The states we have considered so far all share the property that the quasiholes are not the familiar quantum Hall localized anyons, but instead are delocalized objects where the flux associated with them is not directly pinned to the partons. In this section, we consider the more typical localized anyon Ans¨ atze , working in the K0 = 0 .25 case of our generalized Kapit-Mueller model discussed in Sect. III of the main text and Appendix E. This section aims to build confidence in the parton approach, which we do by demonstrating that we are able to accurately construct the entire low-energy subspace of states with 2 or 3 quasiholes.  

> A. Counting of states

We start by reviewing the analytical expectations. Consider an FCI on a torus with Nϕ unit cells (or a Landau Level with Nϕ flux quanta) and N bosons. When Nϕ = 2 N , we have the undoped ν = 12 FCI and here, we consider cases with Nϕ − 2N = Q > 0 quasiholes. How many low-energy states do we expect? By low-energy, we mean below the many-body gap ∆ relative to the ground state — in the FQH case with the model interaction, these states are all degenerate, but in an FCI, they will split by an energy related to the anyon dispersion, denoted by ∼ V in the rest of the paper. The counting is most transparent when we take the FQH thin-torus limit[53], which turns the FCI into a period-2 charge-density wave. The occupation pattern of the ground state is · · · 01010101 · · · and the number of low-energy states is the solution to the counting problem of how many ways there are to occupy N of the Nϕ orbitals arranged on a circle in such a way that no two neighbouring orbitals are both occupied. To insert Q anyons, we start by giving them labels 1 . . . Q and start by placing anyon “1” on any of the Nϕ sites (in the occupation pattern, an anyon can be seen as a double zero, · · · 010100101, where the underlined orbital is taken as the anyon’s location. The next anyon, “2” will be placed at a different position, with 0 , . . . , N bosons between the two – this gives a factor ×(N + 1). For anyon “3”, we again choose between 0 , . . . , N anyons separating it from “1”, but if it happens to fall in the same gap as “2”, we have to specify their ordering, as our anyons are labelled, giving (N + 2) options for anyon “3” and for each following anyon q, we get ( N − 1 + q) further options. Finally, dividing out the Q! anyon permutations, we arrive at the number of states in the low-energy manifold for Q anyons and N

bosons (in Nϕ = 2 N + Q flux) as 

D(Q, N ) = (2 N + Q) × (N + 1) × (N + 2) × . . . × (N + Q − 1) /Q ! = 

N + QQ



+

N + Q − 1

Q



. (S1) The cases we check numerically are D(2 , N ) = ( N + 1) 2 and D(3 , N ) = (2 N + 3)( N + 1)( N + 2) /6. In the FQHE problem, we know how to construct states with localized quasiholes, using the wavefunction χuv in Eq. S1 which constructs holes at u, v . The set of wavefunctions {χu0,v 0 , χ u1,v 1 , . . . , χ uT ,v T } with holes at different positions ( uj , v j ), will, for sufficiently large T > D (2 , N ) (and sufficiently varied positions) form an over-complete basis of two-quasiholes states. We may then compute the Gram matrix Gij = ⟨χui,v i |χuj ,v j ⟩ and the number of non-zero eigenvalues of G

tells us the dimension of the low-energy manifold.  

> B. Construction of local-quasihole states

To carry out this procedure with the parton construction in the FCI case, we must first understand how to construct local-quasihole states in the parton construction. A quasihole at position u corresponds to multiplying the Laughlin wavefunction by Q

> i

(zi − u). One may attempt to construct a parton state by associating this factor to one of the partons, so we would have Ω 1 = Q

> i

(zi − u) Q 

> i<j

(zi − zj ) and Ω 2 = Q 

> i<j

(zi − zj ), but unlike in Sect. IV of the Supplement, here we do not delocalize the quasihole, as there is not an integral over u. This then implies that the 2density of the two partons is different – n1(z) → | z − u|2 as z → u, while n2 remains constant. This goes against the constraint f † 

> 1

f1 = f † 

> 2

f2, which we hope to enforce at least in an averaged sense. To fix this discrepancy, we can insert a half flux quantum π of the internal gauge field at the point u, which introduces factors Q

> i

(zi − u)− 12 and Q

> i

(zi − u) 12 to the parton wavefunctions. Combining with a particle removed for parton 1 at u, we find ˜Ω1 = ˜Ω2 = Q

> i

√zi − u Q 

> i<j

(zi − zj ), where the product of the two correctly reproduces a Laughlin quasihole, and the two partons have equal density everywhere. There are branch cuts in the parton wavefunctions which reflect the semionic statistics, but the full Ansatz for the wavefunctions is well-defined, as the branch cuts cancel. In practice, we may pick two locations u, v and insert π-fluxes there, resulting in N particles for each parton, with parton 1 seeing N + 2 flux and parton 2 seeing N flux. We have two holes available in parton 1, and we can expect that when solving self-consistently, they will be attracted to the π-fluxes to cancel the Q

> i

(zi − u)− 12

factors which would be present otherwise. So in our solver, we simply impose π-fluxes on some locations and optimize the parton Ansatz , which will in turn remove partons from the appropriate locations. Another argument for why this is the correct construction can be made in the thin-torus limit, where the occupation pattern around an isolated quasihole is · · · 01 01 00 10 10 · · · . Each parton effectively sees half flux, so for each underlined pair of orbitals in this thin-torus limit, the parton only has one orbital. It is then clear to see that a quasihole is the combination of an empty parton orbital and a half-period shift for the parton orbitals to one side, consistent with a π-flux inserted at the quasihole. To summarize, Sect. I A provides a prediction for the number of states in the low-energy manifold of a FQH state (the FCI result is expected to be equal) while in Sect. I B we have argued that to construct these states, we should demand localized π-fluxes at some positions, and the resulting state will have quasiholes localized there. The basis presented here exhausts the set of low-energy eigenstates of the system, which means that any of the itinerant anyon phases discussed in the main text may be written as a superposition of these states — in this way, the discussion in this paper may be re-phrased as asking what form of quasihole wavefunction superposition is favourable, as we briefly touch on in Sect. IV of the Supplement as well.  

> C. Numerical results

We finally show the numerical results. Details of how this is done are exactly as in Ref. [27] and as is reviewed in Appendix E above. Note that to make the enforcement of the parton π-fluxes transparent, we keep the parton mean-field Hamiltonian (before flux insertion) ˜H(p)MF; ij translationally invariant, even though it strictly does not have to be (and we expect that the quality of the state can be improved by relaxing this constraint). The justification is that the results we get this way are already good enough to demonstrate that the parton approach works well in this case. Given Q quasiholes, the π-fluxes are inserted at unit cells {(x1, y 1), . . . , (xQ, y Q)}. For each such configuration, we do a VMC run to optimize the effective parton mean-field Hamiltonian. Varying over all physically distinct configurations (which are inequivalent under permutations of the quasiholes and under centre-of-mass translations) constructs a set of states {| ψ⟩i}, and we decompose each of these states |ψi⟩ into components of different values of the centre-of-mass momentum Q, labelled by |ψQ 

> i

⟩ – even if the state is uniform, Q is a good quantum number due to the translation invariant FCI Hamiltonian. At each Q, we compute the Gram matrix GQ 

> ij

= ⟨ψQ

The number of non-zero eigenvalues tells us the number of orthogonal states we can construct, and the results in Fig. S1 clearly indicate excellent agreement with the expectation. In our previous related work, Ref. [27], we studied a “checkerboard” model of an FCI – while we do not review this model here, the important feature is that it is a two-band model, where the lower C = 1 band is close to flat, and in this model, numerical calculations have confirmed a bosonic ν = 12 FCI, despite the fact that this lower band is not perfectly flat and that it does not posses an ideal or vortexable geometry. Still, in Ref. [27] we have found the parton description to work remarkably well for a range of parameters in the undoped model. Here we extend this analysis to local-quasihole states – in Fig. S2, we repeat the analysis we did for the Kapit-Mueller model in Fig. S1 for this checkerboard model. For the checkerboard model on such small systems, we may take the predicted physical states (the ones corresponding to large λQ

This good agreement with exact diagonalization for the dispersive, non-ideal checkerboard model, for which finding good trial wavefunctions can be very tricky, is highly encouraging. While we do not perform exact diagonalization calculations on the generalized Kapit-Mueller model, the combination of this high overlap for the checkerboard model and the fact that our construction is exact in the limit of a Landau Level should give confidence that our results for this generalized KM model are correct to high accuracy, as this model shares more features with the LL than the checkerboard does (in particular, the generalized KM model has an ideal band geometry). 

II. CHERN-SIMONS RESPONSE THEORY FOR GAPPED STATES AT ν = 13 − δ

In the main text, we saw that when doping the ν = 13 state such that the quasiholes are at fillings ˜ ν = ( −3, −3, 0), the resulting state is a superconductor. In this section, we demonstrate that whenever all ˜ ν are integers, the same 4conclusion results. The projective translation symmetry for the partons implies three degenerate maxima in each parton’s dispersion, meaning that if we demand that an integer number of mLL’s is filled in each valley (to achieve a state which is gapped and does not break translational symmetry), all ˜ ν must be multiples of three. But the superconducting conclusion arises more generally, even if they are not, for example, by the translation symmetry being broken, which could lead to a unique maximum for the parton dispersions. So all ˜ ν being multiples of three guarantees a gapped mean-field Ansatz , and (independently) a gapped Ansatz and all ˜ ν being integers leads to a superconducting state. So together, ˜ ν all being multiples of three on its own guarantees superconductivity, while if ˜ν are all integers, superconductivity is a possibility if the parton mean-field can become gapped, for example via a spontaneous breaking of the translational symmetry. Upon doping a density δ of holes, denote the fluxes seen by the partons with bp with the constraint P 

> p

bp = 0. Each parton on average sees δ + bp/2π quasiholes in flux −bp, leading to a filling fraction ˜ νp = −2πδ/b p − 1. The gapped states happen when all bp̸ = 0, so we may set ( L, M, N ) = ( −2πδ/b 1, −2πδ/b 2, −2πδ/b 3) where we require L, M, N 

integer (to ensure integer hole fillings) and also that P 

> p

bp = 0, which leads to LM + M N + N L = 0. The partons fill C = 1 bands with holes at fillings −1 + ( L, M, N ) on top, meaning that Chern number of the gapped partons are 

C = ( L, M, N ) and the gaps are ∆ ∼ (1 /|L|, 1/|M |, 1/|N |). Assuming a U(1) × U(1) invariant gauge group for now, we may write the effective theory, assigning partons to be charged under ( a1, a 2 − a1, A − a2)

L = 14π aI KIJ da J − 12π tI aI dA + N

L + M −M

−M M + N



(S1) Because det K = 0, there exists a zero eigenvalue and the combination of gauge fields associated with that eigenvalue will lead to superconductivity. We may parametrize all solutions to the Diophantine equation LM + M N + N L = 0 as k, u, v ∈ Z with gcd( u, v ) = 1 and L = k(u + v)u, M = −kuv, N = k(u + v)v. Applying the transformation 

W =

 v −u



∈ SL (Z, 2) with vσ = 1 + uρ (such σ, ρ ∈ Z always exist if gcd( u, v ) = 1) by letting K → W KW T ,

t → W t and a → W a , we find that with ˜ a1 = va 1 − ua 2 and ˜ a2 = σa 2 − ρa 1,

L = k

4π ˜a2d˜a2 − kv (u + v)σ

2π ˜a2dA + Q

2π ˜a1dA (S2) Because there is no quadratic term for ˜ a1, this Higgses the external electromagnetic field A and leads to a supercon-ductor. The order parameter is the monopole operator M˜a1 and it has charge Q = kuv (u + v), which is re-assuringly always an even number. While a superconductor exists for any k, u, v ∈ Z if gcd( u, v ) = 1, we can expect the most important cases to be ones where the gap is larger, which translates to small |L|, |M |, |N |, meaning that smaller k, u, v 

generally give lower-energy states. One case is u = −2, v = 1 , k = −1 which gives ˜ ν = ( −3, −3, 0) and is the translationally invariant superconductor discussed in the main text (up to parton permutations). Because two of the partons have identical ˜ ν and arise from an identical underlying band, they may have an identical mean-field Ans¨ atze which would in turn imply a U(2) gauge invariance. If there is U(2) gauge invariance in the ˜ ν = ( −3, −3, 0) state, the resulting central charge is c− = 52 , and the state is related to one of the constructions in Ref. [46] of states achieved by tuning bandwidth. We note that U(2) is the largest gauge group that these gapped states can have when starting from m = 3 — the cases where the larger SU(3) remains unbroken are not gapped at the mean field level as discussed in Sect. IV in the main text. Another state worth pointing out is u = −2, v = 1 , k = 1 with ˜ ν = (1 , 1, −2). Because f1, f 2 have the same Chern number (and both arise from the same underlying band, doped in the same way), they might again have an identical mean-field Ansatz , leading to U(2) gauge invariance with the SU(2) component acting on ( f1, f 2). We note that this state has C = (2 , 2, −1), so the response is the time-reversal conjugate of the ˜ ν = ( −3, −3, 0) state which has C = ( −2, −2, 1). From this, we expect c− = +3 /2 for the ˜ ν = (1 , 1, −2) state, which, after particle-hole conjugating the entire Landau level, gives a doped ν = 23 + δ state which is a charge-2 superconductor with central charge ˜ c− = 1 − 32 = − 12 . This agrees with the value inferred based on pairing symmetry in Ref. [26]. But since ˜ ν

are not multiples of m = 3 in this state, it likely breaks translational symmetry. But despite this, we argue that the effects of this symmetry breaking may be relatively mild, in particular that they may only be of O(δ2) for a density δ

of quasiholes. To understand this, note that the ˜ ν = (1 , 1, −2) Ansatz has quasihole densities δ/ 2, δ/ 2 and 2 δ. Out of the three degenerate peaks available in the dispersion for each parton (see Fig. 7), put all the quasiholes arising from partons 1,2 onto the first (orange) peak while splitting parton 3’s quasiholes among the other two peaks. While such a construction only respects T 3 

> x

for any given parton, summing over the partons cancels these deviations to lowest order (as each peak has a density δ of holes), while the different effective fields seen by the partons mean that the profiles do not cancel perfectly at O(δ2). While our numerics have not identified scenarios where this state would be favourable, it is worth noting its existence due to the DMRG results of [26]. 5      

> III. ANYON INTERPRETATION OF THE SUPERCONDUCTOR FROM ν=13−δ

The U(2) anyon superconducting state we found when hole-doping the ν = 13 FCI at ˜ ν = ( −3, −3, 0) analytically in Sect. II C and within VMC in Sect. III deserves more discussion. Its existence is rather surprising in light of the expectation that since three Q = 13 anyons can form a microscopic fermion, there is no reason to combine further anyons into Cooper pairs. We attempt an explanation of this in the “stack and condense” picture developed in Ref. [15, 54]. We first give the rough outline of the logic, followed by a more detailed calculation in Sect. III A. We recall that the starting point is the Laughlin state which may be described by U(2) −1,−6 Chern-Simons theory, where writing U(2) = [SU(2) × U(1)] /Z2, we can follow [15, 46] to define U(2) k1,k 2 = [SU(2) k1 × U(1) k2 ]/Z2, which is a valid theory if 2 k1 + k2 ∈ 4Z [it is bosonic (fermionic) if (2 k1 + k2)/4 is even (odd)]. This U(2) −1,−6 theory has the same anyon content as the more usual U(1) 3 picture. Our construction then “stacks” a neutral U(2) 3,6 topological order created by the quasiholes on top of this. This order is generated by three anyon types, h, c, τ , with fusion rules 

h3 = I, c 2 = I, τ 2 = I + τ . At finite anyon density, the degeneracy of the two fusion channels is lifted and we assume the “paramagnetic” I is favoured over τ [15, 54] (the contrary leads to a spontaneous breaking U(2) → U(1) × U(1) and ultimately an imbalance of the mean-field flux between f1, f 2). Denoting the charge e/ 3 anyon in U(2) −1,−6 by q,we argue that the elementary charged excitation of the stacked theory is ¯ qv with v = hcτ . Physically, we may picture that each Q = 13 quasiparticle introduced into the system in the U(2) −1,−6 order splits into (1) a charged part and (2) the neutral anyon v = hcτ . Combining three quasiholes gives a unit charge and v3 → cτ which is a non-trivial anyon (assuming τ × τ → I). To be able to fuse into the identity, we require at least v6, meaning that one needs to combine 6 original Laughlin quasiholes to create an energetically favourable local excitation in the stacked order, implying that the lowest-charge local excitation in the low-energy theory is in fact the charge-2 e Cooper pair. While this could be part of the explanation, we note that it is presently not clear how this translates to the algebraic theory of anyon condensation — while a clear correspondence between anyon superconductivity and anyon condensation exists for Abelian CS theory, the mapping in the non-Abelian case appears more complicated [46]. With the above picture outlined, we now turn to a more detailed calculation.  

> A. Details of the stack-and-condense calculation

We follow [15, 54] closely, starting with the undoped ν = 13 FCI which we choose to describe by a U(2) gauge field α

– note that in contrast to Eq. 9 of the main text, the gauge field α here is U(2) and not SU(2), meaning that we allow for Tr α̸ = 0 instead of introducing an additional field a, the reason being that this unified picture, while not separating the charge and neutral sectors as neatly, correctly accounts for the Z2 quotient in U(2) = [U(1) × SU(2)] /Z2, which is a detail we mostly ignored in the main text, as the conclusions drawn there were independent of it. The partons (f1, f 2) form the ( j, n ) = (1 /2, 1) representation of U(2) while f3 is in the (0 , −2), making ψ = f1f2f3 a singlet as needed. In the undoped case, all the partons are in gapped bands with Cp = 1 and we get the theory 

L1/3 = 14π Tr

The resulting theory has three anyons, I, q, q 2 with q3 = I and where q, q 2 both have topological spin θq = e−2πi/ 3. The Q = 13 anyons in our theory are sourced by a combination of q and the parton f1,2 giving the full excitation spin e2πi/ 6 as is familiar from U(1) 3.Now consider introducing a finite density of quasiholes, and assume we are in the intermediate case which leads to partons at filling ˜ ν = ( −3, −3, 0). The matter field associated with these holes so far has been f ∼ (f1, f 2), but now we perform another partonization f = ΦΨ where Φ is a boson and Ψ a fermion. The idea is to first consider the theory with Φ gapped (the “stacking”) and then condense Φ, where we note that if Φ is condensed, f and Ψ are the same. Thus the final theory after this procedure will be exactly as discussed, and the introduction of Φ is just an analytical device to separate the stacking and condensation parts of the procedure. The additional partonization introduces a new gauge field, β, which we take to be U(2) as well. We let Ψ transform in the fundamental rep. of β (Ψ → Uβ Ψ), which means that Φ transforms under gauge transformations as Φ → UαΦU †

as required. If Φ condenses, the Higgs mechanism necessitates that α = β. Before proceeding, we need to choose a mean-field Ansatz . To be able to condense Φ later on, we choose ⟨α⟩ = ⟨β⟩, meaning that Ψ (the U(2) fermion) is at filling ˜ ν = −3. Integrating Ψ out (it is assumed gapped), we arrive at

(S2) 6Ignoring the matter field for now, this has stacked the original (charged) U(2) −1,−6 order with another (charge-neutral) topological order (described by β) which in this notation is U(2) 3,6. Before proceeding with the condensation, we describe the anyon content of U(2) 3,6 = [SU(2) 3 × U(1) 6]/Z2. The anyons in SU(2) 3 × U(1) 6 are labelled as ( j, n ) by the SU(2) spin 0 ≤ j ≤ 3/2 and U(1) charge 0 ≤ n < 6. There are no self-bosons in this theory, but (3 /2, 3) is a fermion and the Z2 quotient describes stacking with the trivial fermionic theory {1, ˜c} and condensing J = ˜ c × (3 /2, 3) which is a bosonic simple current with J2 = I.The resulting theory has 12 particle types [55] and is generated by h, c, τ where h3 = I, c 2 = I and τ × τ = I + τ . In terms of U(2) representations ( j, n ), we have h = (0 , 2), c = (3 /2, 3) and τ = (1 , 0) where the Z2 quotient necessitates 

j + n/ 2 ∈ Z. From these assignments, we see that Φ, which transforms in the anti-fundamental representation (1 /2, 5) of U(2), sources the anyon v = hcτ in this topological order. But note that Φ also transforms in the fundamental rep. of U(2) −1,−6 and as such sources the charge- 13 Laughlin anyon ¯ q. When Φ condenses (by an appropriate choice of L[Φ; α, β ]), the physical picture is that upon doping an anyon q into the system, this anyon gives its charge to the Φ condensate, which makes it transition into a neutral anyon in the stacked order, q → q(¯ qhcτ ) = hcτ .To continue the analysis, we consider the fusion rule τ × τ = I + τ . It has been argued in Ref. [15] that at a finite anyon density, the two channels cease to be degenerate. While the favoured channel depends on energetics, it appears more natural that the “paramagnetic” I [carrying j = 0 under SU(2)] is favoured over the “ferromagnetic” 

τ (carrying j = 1), with the argument coming from a calculation similar to that of Sect. VI. Assuming that the 

I is indeed favoured, we see that introducing three quasiholes leads to v3 ∼ cτ , which is a non-trivial anyon (the fermion c, together with the anyon τ ). To create a local excitation, we must introduce six quasiholes as v6 → I.But such an excitation comprising of six quasiholes is a charge-2 e boson, the Cooper pair. If instead the τ channel was favoured, v3 ∼ c is a fermion, and this means that three quasiholes can lead to a local fermionic excitation (the electron). In that case, superconductivity might not be expected – indeed, if the τ channel is favoured [due to the finite-j representations of SU(2)], the flux seen by f1, f 2 would differ, which contradicts our numerical results in that regime, where equality of the mean-field fluxes, b1 = b2, is favoured. Thu,s our numerical results are consistent with the above, I case (and resulting Cooper pairs) and not this alternative τ scenario with low-energy fermions. But because ¯ qhcτ is not a bosonic simple current, it is not clear how to interpret the above argument in terms of anyon condensation. One would need anyon condensation to lead from U(2) −1,−6 × U (2) 3,6 to U(2) 2,0 which has no residual anyons. A more detailed analysis would be required to understand the connection between the two. Finally, we note the connection between our theory and the anyon superconductor of Ref. [54], where it is obtained by stacking U(1) 2 × U(1) −6 on top of U(1) 3 order. In our construction, start by considering the fully polarized case with filling fractions ˜ ν = ( − 32 , 0, 0). We have so far thought of this theory as a “Secondary CFL” following [14], but other gapped states are possible. Denoting the quasihole creation operator by ψ (recall that it couples to a gauge field a), let us do another partonization ψ = Φ φΨ where Φ , φ are bosons coupling to a − α − β and α and Ψ is a fermion coupling to β with α, β being new gauge fields associated with this partonization. The idea is again to demand condensation of Φ, meaning that we look for gapped mean fields where ⟨a⟩ = ⟨α + β⟩. One such choice is 

α = 3 a/ 4 and β = a/ 4 which allows us to put φ into the gapped n = −2 bosonic IQHE state (which is only trivially gapped if translation symmetry is broken) and Ψ into the n = −6 fermionic IQHE. The resulting theory is 

L = 24π ada + 14π adb + 24π bdb + L[Φ , a − α − β] − 24π αdα − 64π βdβ, (S3) showing that we are stacking U(1) 2 × U(1) 6. Here, condensation of Φ has the clear interpretation of stacking the anyon ¯ q with s (the semion in U(1) 2) and ρ (from U(1) 6 with α6 = I). The composite ¯ qsρ is a charged self-boson and may be condensed, which confines all remaining anyons and leads to a superconducting state. Note that here, introducing three q quasiholes leads to q3(¯ qsρ)3 = ρ3̸ = I which is an anyon, demonstrating that the lowest-charge local excitation consists of 6 quasiholes, i.e., it is the charge-2 e Cooper pair. The slight difference between the descriptions arises because [54] begins with U(1) 3 CS theory (with anyons sourced by a bosonic field), while our starting point is the U(1) 2 theory with K =

2 1 1 2

, where the anyons are sourced by fermionic fields (the partons). To make the connection more explicit, one may perform a 3D XY duality replacing Φ with a U(1) gauge field c in Eq. S3 as L[ϕ, a − α − β] → 12π cd [a − α − β].  

> IV. THE EFFECT OF INTERACTIONS

In this section, we expand on our argument in Sect. II E on how particle interactions may discriminate between different candidate states. We start by focusing on the simplest example of two quasiholes in the ν = 12 bosonic FCI 7where we partonize ψb = f1f2. In a generalized Landau Level, the wavefunctions 

χuv ({zi}) = Y

(S1) represent an un-normalized, overcomplete basis of all two-quasihole states below the many-body gap, where u, v ∈ C

represent the locations of the quasiholes in the complex plane. As a consequence, all the parton states we consider can be expressed as ψ = R d2ud 2v g (u, v )χuv . In this language, states with different mean-field fluxes bp considered in Sect. II C correspond to different allowed forms of g(u, v ) [which can be related to a quasihole pseudowavefunction]. The implications of the different g(u, v ) on the kinetic energy terms have already been discussed in Sect. II C and here, we discuss the effect different g(u, v ) have on the interaction terms. In the quantum Hall problem without a kinetic term for the quasiholes, we expect the holes to localize and to have 

g(u, v ) ∼ f (u − u0)f (v − v0), with f (z) some function peaked around the origin which decays on a scale comparable to the magnetic length. For large |u0 − v0|, the two quasiholes are always well separated and interactions between them do not play a significant role. The situation changes when we introduce a dispersion for the quasiholes in an FCI — now, the kinetic term makes it favourable for the quasiholes to delocalize, and have a non-zero amplitude g(u, v )for u, v throughout the sample. Two such delocalized quasiholes will generally have an amplitude to be close to each other, and it is in this case that the interactions may introduce an energy penalty. To understand the energetics, we need to see how a delocalized g(u, v ) behaves when u → v.In the two-quasihole ν = 12 state, there exist two possibilities for the parton flux – either (A) both partons see equal flux, leading to one hole per parton, or (B) there is a re-distribution of flux, giving two holes to one parton and zero to the other. If we let |LLL N ⟩ denote a filled LLL with N orbitals, we can write down the mean-field parton states 

|Ωp⟩ for the two cases as (A): |Ω1⟩ = fα |LLL N ⟩ , |Ω2⟩ = fβ |LLL N ⟩ (S2) (B): |Ω1⟩ = ˜fα ˜fβ |LLL N +1 ⟩ , |Ω2⟩ = |LLL N −1⟩ (S3) where the f ’s are delocalized parton annihilation operators. Note that all |Ωp⟩ contain exactly N − 1 particles. By writing fα = R d2uf α(u)f (u) (with f (u) the annihilation operator at position u), we get for example Ω (A)1 =

We now make the crucial assumption of quasihole delocalization – that is, for a generic position z, all the orbitals fα,β (z), ˜fα,β (z) have non-zero magnitude. In that case, we see that g(A) (u, v ) ∼ const. as u → v, demonstrating that χu→v,v is represented in Eq. S5 as well as states with the holes well-separated. On the other hand, we can clearly see that g(B) (u, v ) → 0when u → v, even if ˜fα,β are both extended. This shows that χu→v,v is under-represented in Eq. S6, relative to states with the holes further separated, implying a suppression for the amplitude of the quasiholes approaching each other. Assuming that a repulsive interaction between the microscopic particles leads to a repulsive effective interaction between the quasiholes, we then see that such interactions would favour the polarized scenario (B) because it prevents the quasiholes from approaching each other, unlike (A). We note that the delocalization of quasiholes is necessary for this argument to work. FIG. S3. States of the doped ν = 13 − δ fermionic FCI at δ = 118 on a 6 × 6 torus for parameters otherwise identical to Fig. 6. The results and different anyon phases here are consistent with the picture observed at δ = 19 .

When the f ’s are delocalized, however, the forms of g are truly different as described above. Thus, delocalisation of the quasiholes is crucial for this interaction effect to be significant, and we thus do not expect to see any analogous effect in pure quantum Hall physics. A comment on the exchange symmetry of the quasiholes is in order. When adiabatically exchanging two quasiholes in the ν = 12 FCI, we expect to obtain the semionic phase eiπ/ 2, but this phase is not respected in our construction. To rectify this, we first stress that the Ans¨ atze Eq. S5, S6 crucially respect the correct (bosonic) exchange statistics for the actual microscopic particles, meaning they are valid as variational wavefunction forms and their quality should be judged as a function of their energy. But more physically, the absence of this phase may be tied to the charge-flux unbinding of the quasiholes [15]. A true semion would correspond to locally acting with fp(r0) to remove a parton 

and introducing a π-flux of the internal gauge field at the point r0. These fluxes associated with the quasiholes are responsible for the semionic exchange statistics. But in our construction, we imagine a uniform flux of the internal field. The idea is that at a finite density of quasiholes, we may replace the fluctuating fluxes tied to the quasiholes by an average background mean-field, in an approximation exactly analogous to the usual “flux smearing” done in the composite fermion approach to quantum Hall states. Because of this smearing, which is crucial to get anyon-superconducting behaviour, the quasiholes considered in Eq. S5, S6 are not exactly semions (which would be the bound state of a parton hole and a π-flux), but are instead only the “charge” part of this charge-flux composite and as such, they are not expected to obey semionic exchange statistics. Due to this unbinding, the resulting superconductor no longer supports anyons as excitations. The crucial ingredient in this calculation was the fact that the fermionic nature of the parton holes suppresses the amplitude for two such holes to approach each other when the holes arise from the same parton (B). When arising from different partons (A), there exists no such suppression. This principle trivially generalizes to many quasiholes and more species of partons, as explained in Sect. II E. 

V. SMALLER DOPING FRACTION FOR THE FERMIONIC ν = 13 FCI 

In the main text, we considered ν = 13 − δ with δ = 1 /9. In this section, an analogous calculation is performed for 

δ = 1 /18 with the result shown in Fig. S3. The results are consistent with what was discussed in the main text. The model and parameters used are identical to Fig. 6 in the main text. Note that there are fewer possible values of bp

here, as the variation of bp cannot be arbitrarily fine — it is set by the magnetic monopole quantization constraint that 2 πb pNs ∈ Z for a torus with Ns sites. 

VI. NON-ABELIAN CHERN-SIMONS-MAXWELL THEORY 

Our VMC numerical results indicate that there exist systems where the best parton description of the doped state has b1 = . . . = bm = 0 and the mean-field Ansatz preserves the full SU( m) gauge invariance. The partons in 9these states are gapless, and to understand what these states physically correspond to, interactions between partons, mediated by the gauge field, become important. The system may be described as as a small density mδ of quasiholes f † = ( f † 

> 1

, f † 

> 2

, . . . f †

> m

) transforming in the fundamental representation of an SU( m) gauge field aμ. Following [15], the effective theory contains (1) level-1 self-Chern Simons term for aμ as a result of the Laughlin state we are doping on top of, (2) Some action L[f, a ] for the quasiholes coupled to the gauge field and (3) less RG-relevant terms, including a Maxwell-Type term for aμ,

L = − 14π Tr 



ada + 23 a3



+ 12g2 Tr f ∧ ⋆f + L[f, a ] (S1) with f = da + a2. Integrating out the gauge field a will lead to an effective interaction term for f . The combination of the Chern-Simons and Maxwell terms endows a with a mass μ = g2/2π and the resulting interaction potential will decay with separation. The more subtle question is the sign of the interaction. To understand this, let T a

(a = 1 , . . . , m 2 − 1) be the Lie algebra generators of SU( m). The effective two-particle interaction looks like [15] 

U (r) ∼ e−μ|r|

(S2) where the T(1) ,(2) act on the two particles (each of T a is an m × m matrix, and a Kronecker product between the two is understood). The negative eigenvalues of the matrix on the right side (and their eigenvectors) will determine in what states two quasiholes must be to be attracted by this potential. Consider first SU(2). Combining two fundamental (spin- 12 ) particles gives 4 eigenvalues, the singlet at −3 and the triplet at +1. This means that the particles are only attracted when in the singlet channel, and singlet bound states may form. For m = 2, this physically corresponds to pairing ∼ ⟨ f1f2⟩ as discussed in the main text. The resulting bound state is neutral with respect to the SU(2) field and will be invisible to newly added quasiholes. At the level of representation theory, we have 2 × 2 = 1 + 3 and we are evaluating the quadratic Casimir for the irreps on the right side. The same game may be played for SU(3), where 3 × 3 = ¯ 3 + 6 with the anti-fundamental ¯ 3

having lower energy, although it is still charged. Upon adding a third particle, 3×3×3 = 1+8+8+10 with the singlet

(Kronecker products implied), meaning the strongest attractive interaction potential U (r). Again, groups of three quasiholes may be brought together to form a bound singlet state which is then neutral with respect to aμ, meaning that we should not expect the energy to be further lowered by interactions with more particles. We stress that this is merely a tree-level perturbative analysis of the inter-parton interactions mediated by the gauge fields. The many-body parton state will be determined by the competition between interactions of multiple partons and also the competition between kinetic energy and these interactions. Informed by the nature of the interaction, we suggested some possible conclusions in the main text, but a final answer to this question is beyond the scope of this work.
